skill.name: svelte-code-writer
skill.description: Guides Svelte 5 and SvelteKit work by using the @sveltejs/mcp CLI for documentation lookup and component analysis.
skill.summary: Use Svelte MCP documentation and autofix tools to write, review, and validate Svelte components.
skill.author: sveltejs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command matches and two high-signal false positives. The confirmed concern is legitimate but elevated-risk use of npx @sveltejs/mcp commands, including file-path analysis with svelte-autofixer. No evidence found of malicious intent, data exfiltration, credential access, network beacons, or prompt injection attempts in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution Required
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run npx @sveltejs/mcp commands for documentation lookup and code analysis. This is legitimate Svelte tooling, but it executes an external package at runtime and should be used in trusted workspaces with normal command approval controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed commands are explicit npx invocations, so external command execution is confirmed. The package name and documented purpose appear legitimate, and no malicious command chaining or obfuscation is present.

medium_findings.title: Local File Path Analysis
medium_findings.description: The svelte-autofixer command accepts a code string or local path and examples show scanning Svelte component files. This creates filesystem exposure limited to user-selected project files, not hidden or arbitrary secret files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-path workflow is directly documented in the skill. The risk is moderate because the examples target normal Svelte source files and do not instruct broad file reads or secret harvesting.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Match Is False Positive
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography alert points to the metadata description, which only names Svelte file extensions such as .svelte.ts and .svelte.js. No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API usage is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 contains descriptive text about Svelte file types, not cryptographic code. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, cipher configuration, or related weak algorithm usage.

low_findings.title: Hidden File Access Static Match Is False Positive
low_findings.description: The filesystem alert on line 3 appears to be triggered by dot-prefixed Svelte extensions in the description. The skill does not instruct access to hidden files or directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious text is a file extension reference, not a hidden-path access pattern. File analysis appears later, but it targets explicit Svelte component paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: Runtime npx Package Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill depends on npx @sveltejs/mcp commands. Runtime package execution can run downloaded package code, so users should rely on trusted package resolution and normal sandbox or approval settings.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly shown as npx invocations. They are not hidden or obfuscated, but they still execute external package code.

content.user_title: Validate Svelte Code with MCP Tools
content.value_statement: Svelte projects need current framework guidance and component checks. This skill directs Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to use Svelte MCP commands for documentation lookup and autofix review.
actual_capabilities: Lists available Svelte 5 and SvelteKit documentation sections through @sveltejs/mcp.
Retrieves selected documentation sections before editing Svelte code.
Runs svelte-autofixer on inline snippets or local component files.
Checks code against Svelte 4 or Svelte 5 target versions.
Reminds agents to escape rune dollar signs when passing inline code through a shell.
limitations: Requires the @sveltejs/mcp CLI to be available through npx.
Does not replace project tests, type checks, or browser validation.
Focuses on Svelte and SvelteKit files, not general frontend frameworks.
External command execution should follow the user workspace approval model.

use_cases.title: Build Svelte 5 Components
use_cases.description: Use current Svelte documentation and autofixer feedback while creating components with runes and modern syntax.

use_cases.title: Review Svelte Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Check changed .svelte files with the autofixer before approving framework-specific code changes.

use_cases.title: Migrate Legacy Components
use_cases.description: Compare Svelte 4 and Svelte 5 behavior while updating components and validating syntax changes.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Svelte code writer workflow to review this component for Svelte 5 issues and explain the required fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Find Relevant Docs
prompt_templates.prompt: List Svelte documentation sections, choose the sections relevant to stores and runes, then summarize what applies to my task.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a File Path
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the Svelte autofixer on src/lib/Widget.svelte and use the results to propose a minimal patch.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Svelte 4 components, fetch relevant Svelte 5 documentation, and identify migration risks before editing.

output_examples.input: Review a Svelte component that uses runes and event handlers.
output_examples.output: A concise list of framework issues, the documentation sections consulted, and recommended component changes.

output_examples.input: Validate src/lib/Counter.svelte before finalizing a change.
output: Autofixer findings summarized in plain language.
A short explanation of which fixes are required.
A reminder to run project tests after the Svelte-specific review.

output_examples.input: Compare Svelte 4 and Svelte 5 syntax for a migration task.
output_examples.output: A migration-focused summary that separates version differences, likely edits, and remaining manual checks.

best_practices: Fetch only the documentation sections relevant to the current Svelte task.
Run the autofixer before finalizing component edits or review comments.
Use explicit file paths and inspect proposed fixes before applying them.
anti_patterns: Do not run autofixer commands on broad directories without a clear review scope.
Do not treat autofixer output as a replacement for tests or type checks.
Do not pass unescaped rune syntax as inline shell input.

faq.question: Does this skill write Svelte code directly?
faq.answer: It guides an AI assistant to use Svelte documentation and autofixer commands while writing or reviewing code.

faq.question: Which Svelte versions does it mention?
faq.answer: It targets Svelte 5 by default and documents an option for Svelte 4 analysis.

faq.question: Does it require external commands?
faq.answer: Yes. It instructs the assistant to run npx @sveltejs/mcp commands.

faq.question: Can it analyze local files?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented autofixer can analyze a Svelte file path supplied by the user.

faq.question: Does it access secrets or environment variables?
faq.answer: No evidence found in SKILL.md that it reads secrets or environment variables.

faq.question: Should I still run my project checks?
faq.answer: Yes. Use this skill alongside tests, type checks, and browser validation.
